skill.name: arboreto
skill.description: Infer gene regulatory networks from gene expression data with Arboreto, including GRNBoost2, GENIE3, transcription factor filtering, and Dask-based scaling guidance.
skill.summary: Guide Arboreto workflows for scalable gene regulatory network inference from transcriptomics data.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, algorithm names, and scientific terminology. The real risks are legitimate workflow risks: a helper script reads and writes user-specified local files, and distributed Dask examples can connect to remote scheduler endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Remote Dask Scheduler Connection Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents connecting Arboreto jobs to a Dask scheduler address for cluster execution. This is legitimate for distributed bioinformatics work, but expression data may be transmitted to remote workers if users choose an untrusted scheduler.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly show Dask scheduler addresses and client connections. No malicious endpoint is present, but the data movement risk is real when users connect to remote infrastructure.

medium_findings.title: User-Specified Local File Read and Write
medium_findings.description: The bundled script accepts input, output, and transcription factor file paths, then reads expression data and writes network results. This is expected functionality, but users should run it only on intended files and trusted datasets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly reads paths from command line arguments and writes the requested output path. This is not malicious, but it is confirmed filesystem access.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown Code Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur on fenced Markdown examples for Bash or Python usage. They are documentation delimiters and example commands, not hidden command execution inside skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations are Markdown fences or examples displayed to the user. There is no Ruby code or dynamic shell execution in those lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Scientific Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The high severity weak cryptography alerts map to words such as algorithm, Random Forest, network, or scheduling in scientific documentation. No cryptographic primitive, password hashing, encryption, or signature implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed context contains bioinformatics and machine learning terms, not cryptographic code. The static pattern appears to have matched broad keywords outside a security context.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Values Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP address, localhost dashboard URL, and scheduler address are examples for Dask cluster setup. They do not contact an external service unless a user applies them in their own environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are presented as Dask setup examples and dashboard documentation. No automatic network request or data exfiltration logic was found in the skill files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Scheduler Address Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use Client connections and scheduler address strings for Dask cluster execution. Users should ensure the scheduler is trusted before sending expression matrices to workers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented and has a clear security boundary. The risk depends on user-selected infrastructure, not on hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Line File Path Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The script takes file paths from command line arguments and passes them to pandas and output writers. This is normal for analysis tools but should be treated as local filesystem access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The argparse definitions and read/write calls are direct evidence. No path sanitization is present, though the behavior is user-directed and expected.

content.user_title: Infer Gene Regulatory Networks with Arboreto
content.value_statement: Gene regulatory network inference can be slow and difficult to configure for transcriptomics datasets. This skill guides Arboreto workflows with GRNBoost2, GENIE3, TF filtering, and Dask scaling options.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to prepare gene expression matrices for Arboreto input.
Guides GRN inference with GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 examples.
Shows how to restrict inference to transcription factor lists.
Documents local and distributed Dask execution patterns.
Includes a ready-to-run Python script for basic TSV workflows.
Describes output columns and practical filtering approaches.
limitations: It does not validate biological conclusions from inferred networks.
It requires users to provide clean numeric expression data.
Large datasets may require careful memory and cluster configuration.
Remote Dask execution depends on trusted user-managed infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Single-Cell Regulatory Network Discovery
use_cases.description: Infer candidate transcription factor and target relationships from single-cell expression matrices.

use_cases.title: Bulk RNA-Seq TF Filtering
use_cases.description: Run Arboreto with a curated transcription factor list to focus regulatory predictions.

use_cases.title: Cluster-Scale GRN Inference
use_cases.description: Plan Dask scheduler and worker usage for large expression datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Basic Run
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small expression matrix.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run Arboreto on an expression_data.tsv file and save a network_output.tsv file.

prompt_templates.title: Add Transcription Factor Filtering
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a curated transcription factor list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through GRNBoost2 inference using my expression matrix and a file of transcription factor names.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Inference Algorithms
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 for the same dataset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me compare GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 for my transcriptomics dataset and explain when each algorithm is appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Distributed Inference
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Dask-based Arboreto workflow for a large single-cell dataset, including worker sizing and data safety checks.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to infer a network from a TSV expression matrix.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step Arboreto workflow covering input format, GRNBoost2 execution, output naming, and reproducibility settings.

output_examples.input: A user has a transcription factor list and wants focused predictions.
output_examples.output: Guidance on loading the TF list, matching names to expression columns, running inference, and reviewing importance scores.

output_examples.input: A user needs to scale inference beyond one workstation.
output_examples.output: A Dask deployment plan with scheduler connection guidance, worker resource choices, monitoring notes, and trust boundaries.

best_practices: Confirm that expression matrix columns are gene names before running inference.
Set a random seed when comparing results across runs or conditions.
Use trusted Dask schedulers and workers when processing sensitive expression data.
anti_patterns: Do not run inference on uncleaned matrices with nonnumeric expression values.
Do not connect to unknown remote schedulers with private datasets.
Do not treat importance scores as validated regulation without downstream analysis.

faq.question: What data format does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects expression data with observations as rows and genes as columns, usually in a Pandas DataFrame or TSV file.

faq.question: Which algorithm should I start with?
faq.answer: Start with GRNBoost2 for most datasets because it is faster and designed for large-scale inference.

faq.question: Can I use a transcription factor list?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows how to load a TF list and restrict candidate regulators during inference.

faq.question: Does this skill run distributed jobs automatically?
faq.answer: No. It explains Dask options, but users choose whether to create a local client or connect to a scheduler.

faq.question: Is remote cluster execution safe?
faq.answer: It can be safe on trusted infrastructure. Do not send private expression data to untrusted schedulers or workers.

faq.question: Does Arboreto prove biological regulation?
faq.answer: No. It produces candidate regulatory links that need filtering, validation, and biological interpretation.
